docs.title: REPACSS Technical Documentation
docs.text: The Research and Educational Platform for Advanced Computing Support Services (REPACSS) provides High Performance Computing (HPC) and data storage capabilities to the Texas Tech University (TTU) research community. This documentation is your central hub for learning how to access, use, and optimize REPACSS for your academic or scientific work.

docs.title: 🔰 Top Documentation Pages
docs.text:

  • Absolute Beginner’s Guide A simplified, jargon-free introduction to HPC concepts and REPACSS usage.

  • Scheduling Policies & Charges Understand the scheduling system, charge factors, and queue limits.

  • Example Job Scripts Pre-built SLURM job scripts to help you submit your first jobs.

  • Basics of Running Jobs Introduction to job submission, SLURM commands, and job lifecycle.

  • Globus & File Transfers High-speed data transfer using Globus, rsync, or scp.

  • Unix File Permissions Guide to file access control, groups, and permissions in a shared HPC environment.

  • Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Learn how to securely access the cluster using MFA and VPN.

docs.title: Welcome Home: A Beginner’s Guide to Using REPACSS
docs.text:
Welcome to REPACSS! Whether you’re an undergraduate student, a new graduate researcher, or just someone eager to get started with high-performance computing, this guide is for you. Our goal is to walk you through the basics of using the REPACSS cluster — from logging in to running your first job.

To make things easier to understand, we’ll use the analogy of a house. REPACSS is like a shared home. You enter through the front door (logging in), have your own room for small items (your home directory), a few common areas like closets and garages for larger items (project and scratch storage), and a kitchen (compute nodes) where the real work happens.

docs.title: 🔑 Unlocking the Front Door: Logging In
docs.text: Before you can start working on REPACSS, you need to log in to the system using SSH.

docs.title: SSH Login (The Front Door)
docs.text:
To log in from your local terminal:

ssh <your_username>@repacss.hpcc.ttu.edu

If this is your first time connecting, you'll likely be prompted to verify the server's RSA key fingerprint. Type yes to continue. You’ll then enter your password and any additional multi-factor authentication code if your account has MFA enabled.

Note

Login nodes are for setup and light work only — like standing in your foyer. Heavy lifting (computation) should always be done on the compute nodes.

docs.title: 🗄 This House Has Closets: Navigating Storage
docs.text:
REPACSS provides different types of storage spaces based on what you’re doing.

Storage Type Location Purpose Home /home/$USER Permanent space for small files/scripts Scratch /scratch/$USER Fast temporary storage during jobs Project/Group /project/<group> Shared space for team/project data

Your home directory is like your room. Keep only essentials here — it’s backed up and secure. Scratch is like the garage: great for big, messy projects in progress, but regularly cleaned out. Project storage is your shared closet with teammates.

Warning

Files in /scratch may be automatically deleted if left inactive for too long. Always back up important work!

docs.title: 🍳 Cooking with Fire: Running Jobs
docs.text:
Now that you’re inside the house, it’s time to use the kitchen — the compute nodes.

You can’t just start computing from the login node. Instead, you must write and submit a job script to run your program on compute nodes.

docs.title: Example: A Simple Job Script
docs.text:
Create a file called hello.sh:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=hello
#SBATCH --output=hello.out
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --partition=standard

echo "Hello from REPACSS!"

Submit it with:

sbatch hello.sh

Check its status with:

squeue -u $USER

Tip

The --partition flag selects which group of compute nodes to use. Common options include standard, gpu, and debug.

docs.title: 📦 Recipe Book: Software and Modules
docs.text: REPACSS uses a module system to manage software packages. Instead of installing everything yourself, just load what you need!

docs.title: Common Module Commands
docs.text:
module avail            # See available software
module load gcc         # Load a module
module list             # View currently loaded modules
module unload gcc       # Unload a module

If your job needs specific software, include the module load commands at the top of your job script.

Example

To use Python in a job, your script might start with:

module load python

docs.title: 🧠 Helpful Hints and Where to Get Help
docs.text:
Everyone starts somewhere — and nobody knows everything. If you run into trouble:

  • Check this user guide
  • Ask your PI, research group, or lab manager
  • Visit Support to contact the REPACSS team

Tip

When asking for help, include error messages and describe what you were trying to do. The more detail, the better!

docs.title: 🧹 House Rules: Good HPC Citizenship
docs.text:
Because REPACSS is a shared resource, there are a few best practices to follow:

  • Don’t compute on the login node.
  • Use scratch for temporary work, but clean it regularly.
  • Keep jobs short in the debug partition; use standard for production runs.
  • Be respectful of shared storage; avoid hoarding large files.

docs.title: 🏁 Make Yourself at Home
docs.text:
Congratulations! You now understand the basics of REPACSS:

  • You’ve logged in
  • Learned the storage layout
  • Loaded software
  • Written and submitted a job

This is just the beginning — explore more detailed topics in the rest of the documentation as you grow comfortable.

Happy computing — and welcome to the cluster!

docs.title: How do I get an account?
docs.text:
To request access to REPACSS:

  1. Submit a request via the account form at repacss.org
  2. Complete required training to ensure familiarity with system use and policies
  3. Review and accept the usage policy
  4. Wait for approval — you’ll be notified once your access is granted

docs.title: How do I reset my password?
docs.text: Password resets are handled through your eRaider account at eraider.ttu.edu. If you are an ACCESS user, refer to your ACCESS identity portal.

docs.title: How do I submit a job?
docs.text:
sbatch job.sh
sbatch -p zen4 job.sh
sbatch -p h100 job.sh

docs.title: How do I check my job status?
docs.text:
squeue -u $USER
squeue -p zen4
squeue -p h100

docs.title: How do I cancel a job?
docs.text:
scancel <job_id>
scancel -u $USER
scancel -p zen4

docs.title: What are the storage quotas?
docs.text:
• Home: 100 GB per user
  • Scratch: 1 TB per project
  • Archive: Allocated per project basis

docs.title: How do I transfer files?
docs.text:
Warning

Use Globus for large or frequent transfers. Use scp only for quick one-time needs.

# Upload to REPACSS
scp file username@login.repacss.org:/mnt/GROUPID/work/USERID/

# Download from REPACSS
scp username@login.repacss.org:/mnt/GROUPID/work/USERID/file ./destination

docs.title: How do I load software?
docs.text:
module avail
module load <module-name>
module list

docs.title: How do I request new software?
docs.text:
• Contact the REPACSS support team
  • Provide installation instructions and dependencies
  • Specify whether it’s for shared use or personal use
  • Wait for confirmation from the system admin team

docs.title: 📂 File Transfer
docs.text:
Warning

The Globus Connect service may be unreliable. Please use one of the SSH-based alternatives below for secure and consistent file transfers.

REPACSS supports file transfers using standard tools available on most systems. These include SCP, rsync, and SFTP. All tools use SSH for secure authentication.

docs.title: 📦 SCP (Secure Copy)
docs.text: SCP is a simple and fast way to copy files between your local machine and REPACSS.

docs.title: 🔼 Upload a file
docs.text: scp myfile.txt username@login.repacss.ttu.edu:/mnt/DISCL/home/username/

docs.title: 🔽 Download a file
docs.text:
scp username@login.repacss.ttu.edu:/mnt/DISCL/home/username/data.csv ./data.csv

Tip

Use -r to copy directories recursively: scp -r myfolder/ username@login.repacss.ttu.edu:/mnt/DISCL/home/username/

docs.title: 🔁 Rsync (Recommended for Large Transfers)
docs.text: rsync is great for syncing entire folders and resuming interrupted transfers.

docs.title: 🔼 Upload a directory
docs.text: rsync -avh myfolder/ username@login.repacss.ttu.edu:/mnt/DISCL/home/username/myfolder/

docs.title: 🔽 Download from REPACSS
docs.text:
rsync -avh username@login.repacss.ttu.edu:/mnt/DISCL/home/username/project/ ./project/

Tip

Add -P to track progress and allow resuming: 

rsync -avhP myfolder/ username@login.repacss.ttu.edu:/mnt/DISCL/home/username/

docs.title: 🖥️ SFTP (Interactive Terminal)
docs.text: SFTP allows interactive browsing of files on REPACSS.

docs.title: Start session:
docs.text: sftp username@login.repacss.ttu.edu

docs.title: Common SFTP commands:
docs.text:
cd /mnt/DISCL/home/username/
put myfile.txt     # Upload
get output.log     # Download
ls                 # List directory contents
exit               # Close connection

Note

Unlike SCP or rsync, SFTP lets you navigate the remote file structure before transferring.

docs.title: 🪟 Windows Users
docs.text:
Tip

Windows 10+ includes scp, sftp, and ssh in PowerShell by default.

If you prefer graphical interfaces:

  • WinSCP – supports SCP and SFTP
  • FileZilla – easy drag-and-drop transfers

Or use WSL to access Linux-style CLI tools like rsync.

docs.title: 🧭 Tips & Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Always use your eRaider username
  • Use the TTUnet VPN before initiating transfers
  • Make sure file paths are correct, especially when uploading to subdirectories

Note

For large files or many files, prefer rsync over scp due to its error recovery and syncing capabilities.

docs.title: Getting Started at REPACSS
docs.text:
About this page

This page will guide you through the basics of accessing, connecting to, and beginning work on the REPACSS High-Performance Computing (HPC) cluster.

Welcome to REPACSS — Texas Tech University's research and educational platform for advanced computing support services. This guide is tailored to new users who are preparing to run jobs on the cluster for the first time.

docs.title: 🧾 Account Access
docs.text: To use REPACSS resources, you must have a valid TTU or ACCESS account.

docs.title: TTU Users
docs.text:
• Get access from the system admin.
  • Requires a valid ttu.edu email address.
  • Access is typically granted within 2–3 business days.

docs.title: ACCESS Users
docs.text:
Warning

ACCESS users are unable to login to REPACSS System at this stage of the project.

  • Must have an [ACCESS allocation].
  • Contact REPACSS support with your ACCESS project ID and intended use.

Account prerequisites

New users must have their accounts approved before attempting to log in to the cluster.

docs.title: 🔐 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) & VPN
docs.text:
To connect to REPACSS, you must be connected to the TTU GlobalProtect VPN and have MFA enabled.

  • VPN Setup Instructions
  • MFA Configuration Guide

MFA and VPN required

All login attempts from outside TTU’s network must be authenticated through VPN and MFA.

docs.title: 💻 Connecting to the Cluster
docs.text:
Once your account is active and your VPN/MFA setup is complete, connect via SSH:

ssh <your-ttu-username>@login.repacss.ttu.edu

You can also connect via Visual Studio Code:

  • Connecting via VSCode

First-time login tip

On first login, review your disk quota using quota -s and check available modules with module avail.

docs.title: 📁 Transferring Files
docs.text:
You can move data to and from REPACSS using the following methods:

  • Command line: scp, rsync
  • GUI: WinSCP, Cyberduck
  • Globus Transfer Guide

Storage areas

Use /home for persistent scripts, /work for active jobs, and /scratch for temporary data.

docs.title: ⚙️ Environment Setup
docs.text:
REPACSS uses a module system to manage software:

module avail         # See available modules
module load gcc/12   # Example: Load GCC 12
  • Using Modules
  • Available Software
  • Installing Your Own Packages

docs.title: 🧪 Submitting Your First Job
docs.text:
REPACSS uses the SLURM workload manager. Jobs are submitted using a script like:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=test
#SBATCH --output=output.txt
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00

hostname

Submit the job using:

sbatch job.slurm

Monitor your job with:

squeue -u <your-username>
  • SLURM Job Basics
  • Example Job Scripts

docs.title: 📘 Overview
docs.text:
Every file and directory in a Unix system has:

  • An owner
  • A set of permission flags
  • Permissions for:
  • the user (owner)
  • other (everyone else)

You can view permissions using the ls -l command:

ls -l

Example output:

drwx------ 2 bsencer bsencer  2048 Jun 12 2012 private
-rw------- 1 bsencer bsencer  1327 Apr  9 2012 try.f90
-rwx------ 1 bsencer bsencer 12040 Apr  9 2012 a.out
drwxr-xr-x 3 bsencer bsencer  2048 Nov 13 2011 public

docs.title: 🔎 Understanding the Permission Flags
docs.text:
Position Meaning 1 d if directory, - if regular file 2–4 Read (r), Write (w), Execute (x) for user (owner) 5–7 Read, Write, Execute for group (removed from scope) 8–10 Read, Write, Execute for other (world)

Flag meanings:

  • - = flag is not set
  • r = readable
  • w = writable
  • x = executable
  • s = setgid (not used in simplified form)

docs.title: 🧠 Interpreting Examples
docs.text:
• drwx------: directory, full access for owner, no access for others.
  • -rw-------: regular file, readable and writable by owner only.
  • -rwx------: executable file, accessible only by owner.
  • drwxr-xr-x: directory, readable/executable by everyone, writable by owner.

docs.title: 🧰 umask
docs.text:
Controls default permissions for new files. Common values:

umask File Perms Dir Perms 002 rw-rw-r-- rwxrwxr-x 007 rw-rw---- rwxrwx--- 022 rw-r--r-- rwxr-xr-x 027 rw-r----- rwxr-x--- 077 rw------- rwx------

Set this in .bash_profile for it to persist.

docs.title: 🔧 chmod (Change Mode)
docs.text: Used to change permissions.

docs.title: Using Octal Notation
docs.text:
Octal Meaning 0 --- (none) 1 --x (execute) 2 -w- (write) 3 -wx (write+exec) 4 r-- (read) 5 r-x (read+exec) 6 rw- (read+write) 7 rwx (all)

Example:

chmod 755 file
# Sets: rwxr-xr-x

docs.title: Using Symbolic Notation
docs.text:
chmod u+x,go+rx file
# Adds execute to user, read+execute to others
Class Description u user (owner) o other (world) a all (user + other) Operator Meaning + Add permission - Remove permission = Set exact permission Mode Description r read w write x execute X special execute (only if already set)

Example:

chmod -R o+rX directory/

Adds read and execute to others for all directories and executable files within.

docs.title: ✅ Summary
docs.text:
• Use ls -l to check permissions.
  • Use chmod to modify them.
  • Use umask to control defaults for new files.
  • Keep sensitive files private with chmod 600 file.

docs.title: Manage Your Account 🧑‍💻🔐
docs.text:
About this Section 📘

This section provides essential information on how to manage your REPACSS account, update credentials, and ensure secure access to system resources.

To use REPACSS, users must have an active account. Managing your account ensures uninterrupted access to computing resources, data storage, and support tools.

docs.title: Password & Credential Management 🔒
docs.text:
User authentication is critical to secure access. REPACSS supports:

  • 🔄 Secure password updates
  • 🛡️ Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) setup (coming soon)
  • 🗝️ SSH key registration for seamless logins

If you forget your password, contact IT Help Central to reset your TTU password.

docs.title: Support 🆘
docs.text: If you encounter account-related issues or have trouble accessing your dashboard, please contact the REPACSS Support Team.

docs.title: ACCESS Accounts 🌐🔑
docs.text:
Feature Under Development 🚧

Integration with the ACCESS (Advanced Cyberinfrastructure Coordination Ecosystem: Services & Support) is not enabled yet in REPACSS. We're actively working on bringing this feature online to support broader national CI access. Stay tuned!

docs.title: What is ACCESS?
docs.text:
ACCESS is a U.S. National Science Foundation (NSF) program that provides researchers with access to advanced computational resources, support services, and training. REPACSS aims to integrate with ACCESS in the future to:

  • 🌍 Expand national access to REPACSS resources
  • 🤝 Support collaboration across U.S. institutions

If you're already an ACCESS user and interested in future integration testing or early access, please contact us:

📧 repacss-support@ttu.edu

docs.title: Password and Account Protection
docs.text:
Users are issued a username (login name) and password to access REPACSS systems. This combination is strictly for individual use only — do not share your password with others. Sharing passwords is a direct violation of REPACSS policy and may result in suspended access.

Passwords must be changed immediately upon suspected compromise or exposure. If you believe your account is compromised, report it promptly to the REPACSS support team at repacss-support@ttu.edu.

docs.title: For TTU eRaider Users:
docs.text:
If your account is tied to your TTU eRaider login, follow these steps to reset your password:

  1. Go to the TTU eRaider Password Reset page.
  2. Click "Forgot Password?".
  3. Provide the required information (e.g., eRaider username and date of birth).
  4. Follow the instructions sent to your registered email address.

If you experience issues with this process, contact the TTU IT Help Central: - ☎️ Phone: 806-742-HELP (4357) - 🌐 Website: https://www.depts.ttu.edu/itts/helpcentral

docs.title: For Local REPACSS Accounts:
docs.text:
If your account is managed locally and not linked to TTU eRaider:

  • Email repacss-support@ttu.edu
  • Provide your username and details about the issue
  • A temporary reset token or assistance will be provided during business hours

docs.title: TTU eRaider Accounts:
docs.text:
1. Log in to https://eraider.ttu.edu
  2. Select Change Password
  3. Choose a new secure password that meets TTU standards

docs.title: Local REPACSS Accounts:
docs.text: Send an email request to repacss-support@ttu.edu and a system administrator will assist you in resetting your password securely.

docs.title: Password Requirements ✅
docs.text:
All REPACSS passwords must follow the standards below:

Requirement Details 🔒 Minimum Length 12 characters 🔤 Character Mix Uppercase, lowercase, number, symbol 🔁 Expiration Every 12 months 🚫 No reuse or common words Avoid names, dates, or easy patterns

Use a passphrase!

Try something memorable and strong like: RedRaider@2024!CloudsGoHigh

docs.title: Login Failures 🚫
docs.text:
Accounts are temporarily locked after 15 failed login attempts. This lock is automatically cleared after 15 minutes — no reset is necessary unless you've forgotten your password.

If you're still unable to log in after that time, reach out to support.

docs.title: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) 🔐
docs.text: Currently, MFA is not required for REPACSS. However, if you are using eRaider or accessing through TTU's VPN and services, MFA will apply at the campus level.

docs.title: Need Help? 🤝
docs.text:
📩 Email: repacss-support@ttu.edu 💻 Visit: https://www.depts.ttu.edu/itts/helpcentral 📍 In person: Engineering Center, TTU Campus

Stay safe, and protect your account!

docs.title: Account Policies 📜🔐
docs.text:
About this Page

This page outlines the core account usage policies that apply to all REPACSS users at Texas Tech University. Following these rules ensures safe, secure, and responsible use of HPC resources.

docs.title: 👤 User Account Ownership, Password, and MFA Policies
docs.text:
Every user is assigned a unique username, which is protected by a personal password and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) token. These credentials are private and must not be shared under any circumstance.

  • 🔑 Passwords must be strong and changed periodically. See the Passwords page for more.
  • 🔐 MFA tokens must be securely stored and are tied to individual accounts.
  • ❌ Account sharing is strictly prohibited. If multiple users are detected accessing a single account, that account will be suspended.

Sharing is a Violation

If someone other than the assigned user accesses a REPACSS account, all associated accounts will be disabled immediately. Reinstatement requires a formal justification from the PI.

docs.title: 🔐 Security Incidents
docs.text:
If you suspect a security breach or account misuse, report it immediately to:

  • 📧 repacss-support@ttu.edu

Include any available logs, screenshots, or timestamps when reporting an incident. Prompt action helps protect everyone.

docs.title: 🆕 Account Creation
docs.text:
• Accounts must be requested through an official research project.
  • New users are vetted by the REPACSS team before access is granted.
  • Setup includes MFA registration and password initialization.

docs.title: ✅ Account Continuation
docs.text:
• Accounts remain active only if associated with an active TTU project.
  • Users must re-accept updated Policies and Code of Conduct documents when prompted.

docs.title: ❌ Account Deactivation
docs.text:
Accounts can be deactivated under several conditions:

Reason What Happens 🔄 End of project allocation Login disabled, data access only for 60 days 🧑‍🔧 User-initiated Immediate deactivation 👨‍🏫 PI removes user Immediate deactivation 🕐 Project membership expiration Immediate deactivation 📜 Policy agreement not signed Account suspended until accepted ⚠️ Security violation Immediate deactivation

After deactivation: - Passwords and MFA tokens are deleted. - Data may remain accessible for a limited period through transfer nodes only.

docs.title: 📣 Acknowledge Use of REPACSS Resources
docs.text:
Why Acknowledgment Matters 📄✨

Proper acknowledgment of REPACSS in your publications helps demonstrate the impact of our research infrastructure, supports funding renewals, and shows the broader academic value of our cluster.

If you publish work that involved the use of REPACSS compute resources, please include the following acknowledgment:

This research used resources of the REPACSS high-performance computing system at Texas Tech University, supported in part by the National Science Foundation under NSF Award No. 2404438 and Texas Tech's High Performance Computing Center (HPCC).

Including this sentence in your papers, posters, or talks helps ensure continued investment in high-performance computing resources and encourages further research support.

Not sure if you should include this?

If your work ran any code, training, or simulations on REPACSS — even during testing — please include the acknowledgment.

docs.title: Texas Tech University (TTU) 🏫🐘
docs.text:
About this Section 📘

This section outlines the role of Texas Tech University in hosting and supporting the REPACSS platform, including local access details and infrastructure support.

docs.title: On-Campus Access 🧑‍🏫📶
docs.text:
REPACSS is physically hosted on TTU’s research infrastructure and can be accessed:

  • 📍 On campus using wired Ethernet or TTUnet Wi-Fi
  • 🌐 Off campus via TTU VPN (VPN Setup Guide)
  • 🔒 Securely through SSH or web interfaces where applicable

VPN Required for All Users

All users must be connected to the TTU network to access REPACSS. This is typically achieved by using GlobalProtect VPN when off-campus. 🧭 However, users in the Computer Science Department building may experience access issues even when connected to TTUnet (campus Wi-Fi). ✅ In such cases, it is recommended to use GlobalProtect VPN as a workaround to ensure proper access.

docs.title: Research & Training Support 🧪📚
docs.text:
REPACSS supports a wide range of computational research and student training programs at TTU, including:

  • 👩‍🔬 Graduate & Undergraduate Research Projects
  • 🧰 Engineering and Computer Science Labs
  • 🖥️ Parallel programming, simulation, and modeling coursework
  • 🧑‍🏫 Faculty-led workshops and technical bootcamps

docs.title: Collaboration Opportunities 🤝
docs.text:
Faculty and students at TTU are encouraged to contribute to the development and expansion of REPACSS:

  • 📬 Propose feature enhancements via GitHub
  • 🧪 Pilot new curriculum modules
  • 💬 Share feedback with REPACSS maintainers and developers

docs.title: Local Support Team 🛠️
docs.text:
Have a question or need technical assistance? The TTU REPACSS team is here to help!

  • 📧 Email: repacss-support@ttu.edu

docs.text:
Before you can connect to REPACSS, ensure the following:

repacss.ttu.edu

docs.title: "Permission Denied" or "Too Many Authentication Failures"
docs.text:
Possible causes:

  • ❌ You mistyped your eRaider username or password.
  • ❌ You're not connected to the TTU network or VPN.
  • ❌ Your SSH client is trying too many keys. Add IdentitiesOnly=yes.

Example:

ssh -o IdentitiesOnly=yes <your_eRaider_username>@repacss.ttu.edu

docs.title: SSH Host Key Changed Warning
docs.text:
If you see:

WARNING: REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!

Do the following:

  1. Open ~/.ssh/known_hosts
  2. Remove the line associated with repacss.ttu.edu
  3. Retry connecting and verify the new host fingerprint if prompted

docs.title: Still Can't Connect?

  • 💬 Include terminal output with -vvv for verbose SSH logging

docs.title: Visual Studio Code
docs.text: Visual Studio Code is a modern, lightweight IDE (Integrated Development Environment) that supports remote development through SSH. It is highly customizable and offers a wide range of extensions for languages and workflows common in HPC environments, such as Python, C/C++, Fortran, and more.

docs.title: Connecting with VSCode Remote SSH
docs.text:
Connecting to REPACSS via the Remote - SSH extension allows you to edit files, run terminals, and debug code directly on the system — just like you would on your local machine.

By default, VSCode will connect you to a REPACSS login node, which is suitable for lightweight tasks like editing code or running small programs. For heavy workloads, you should request access to a compute node using Slurm.

Warning

If your home directory on REPACSS exceeds its quota, VSCode Remote SSH connections may silently fail. Be sure to check your usage and offload files if needed.

docs.title: SSH Configuration for REPACSS
docs.text:
To streamline your VSCode connection, add the following to your ~/.ssh/config file on your local machine:

# Login node configuration
Host repacss
    HostName repacss.hpcc.ttu.edu
    User your_ttu_username
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/repacss
    IdentitiesOnly yes
    ForwardAgent yes
    LogLevel QUIET

If you're on Windows, you can add the same block in: 

C:\Users\YourUsername\.ssh\config

Make sure your SSH key is added to your ssh-agent and that your eRaider account has VPN and MFA enabled (see VPN Setup and MFA Setup).

docs.title: VSCode Settings
docs.text:
To ensure smooth operation on compute nodes, add the following settings to your VSCode settings.json:

"remote.SSH.maxReconnectionAttempts": 2,
"remote.SSH.useFlock": false

This prevents file-locking issues and handles session timeouts when your compute allocation ends.

docs.title: Requesting a Compute Node
docs.text:
Once connected to the REPACSS login node via VSCode or terminal:

salloc --nodes=1 --ntasks=1 --time=01:00:00 --partition=standard

This requests a compute node for 1 hour on the standard partition.

Wait for the job to be granted, and note the node name (e.g., rpc-97-1):

salloc: Nodes rpc-97-1 are ready for job

docs.title: Connect to the Compute Node
docs.text:
Now, back in your SSH config, you can optionally create a direct host entry:

Host repacss-compute
    HostName rpc-97-1
    ProxyJump repacss
    User your_ttu_username
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/repacss
    IdentitiesOnly yes
    ForwardAgent yes
    LogLevel QUIET

Then in VSCode, press F1, choose Remote-SSH: Connect to Host, and pick repacss-compute.

Tip

Once connected to the compute node, you can compile or run heavier jobs without overloading the login node.

docs.title: ⚙️ Compiler Flags
docs.text:
Warning

This page is still under development. Use these recommendations as a starting point and verify the compiler, versions and performance improvements through testing.

Choosing the right compiler flags can significantly impact the performance of your applications on REPACSS. This page provides guidance for commonly used compilers.

docs.title: 🧵 General Tips
docs.text:
• Always use optimization flags (e.g., -O2 or -O3) for performance.
  • Use architecture-specific flags to take advantage of REPACSS hardware.
  • Profile before and after applying flags to measure impact.

docs.title: 🧰 GCC (12.2.0)
docs.text:
-O3 -march=native -funroll-loops -ffast-math -fopenmp
  • -O3 enables aggressive optimizations.
  • -march=native targets the local CPU architecture.
  • -funroll-loops can improve performance in some loops.
  • -fopenmp enables OpenMP support.

docs.title: 💡 Intel OneAPI (2023.0)
docs.text:
-O3 -xHost -qopenmp -fp-model fast=2
  • -xHost optimizes for the highest instruction set available on the host.
  • -qopenmp enables OpenMP parallelism.
  • -fp-model fast=2 relaxes IEEE compliance for faster math.

docs.title: 🚀 NVIDIA HPC SDK (23.1)
docs.text:
-O3 -mp=gpu -gpu=cc90,fastmath -Minfo=accel
  • -mp=gpu enables GPU acceleration with OpenACC.
  • -gpu=cc90,fastmath targets H100 (CUDA compute capability 9.0).
  • -Minfo=accel reports what loops are offloaded.

docs.title: 🧪 Example Usage
docs.text:
GCC:

gcc -O3 -march=native -fopenmp mycode.c -o mycode

Intel:

icx -O3 -xHost -qopenmp mycode.c -o mycode

NVIDIA:

nvc -O3 -mp=gpu mycode.c -o mycode

docs.title: 🧭 Next Steps
docs.text: After compiling, use Profiling Tools and Scaling Tests to evaluate performance.

docs.title: 🛠️ Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text:
Warning

This page is still under development. The content below may be incomplete or subject to revision.

Use this page to identify and resolve common issues encountered on REPACSS.

docs.title: ❓ Job Not Starting
docs.text:
• Check if your job is pending due to resource availability:
squeue --me
  • Look for the NODELIST(REASON) column for hints like:
  • Resources: Not enough nodes available.
  • Priority: Waiting for higher-priority jobs to clear.
  • Dependency: Job depends on another that hasn’t finished.

docs.title: 🧾 Invalid Account or Partition
docs.text:
If you see: 

sbatch: error: Job request does not match any supported policy.

  • Ensure your account/project is valid and available for the selected partition.
  • Set your account using:
#SBATCH --account=m1234

docs.title: 🐍 Python Module Not Found
docs.text:
If you get: 

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'numpy'

  • Check that you’ve activated the correct conda environment: 

    conda activate myenv

  • If it’s missing, reinstall it: 

    pip install numpy

docs.title: 🔗 SSH Connection Issues
docs.text:
• If login is slow or stuck:

  • Try verbose output to debug: 

    ssh -vvv your_username@repacss.ttu.edu

  • Make sure VPN is connected properly.

docs.title: 🔍 "command not found"
docs.text:
If a common command like gcc, python, or nvcc fails: - Check if the required module is loaded: 

module list
module load gcc/12.2.0

docs.title: 📉 Poor Performance
docs.text:
• Check if you’re using correct compiler flags and parallel settings.

  • Use profiling tools like: 

    time ./a.out
perf stat ./a.out

  • Review CPU or GPU utilization via sstat or jobstats.

docs.title: 🧼 Disk Quota or Storage Issues
docs.text:
If you see: 

Disk quota exceeded

  • Use du -sh * to identify large files.
  • Remove or move unnecessary data.

docs.title: 📤 Data Transfer Fails
docs.text:
• If scp or rsync fails:
  • Double-check path and permissions.
  • For large transfers, consider: 
    rsync -avzP source/ user@repacss.ttu.edu:/destination/

docs.title: 🔁 Infinite Loop or Hanging Job
docs.text:
• Make sure loops have valid exit conditions.
  • Add logging or print statements to verify execution flow.
  • You can cancel the job with: 
    scancel <jobid>

docs.title: 🧩 Module Conflicts
docs.text:
• If loading a module breaks something: 
    module purge
module load only_what_you_need

docs.title: 📫 Need Help?
docs.text:
If all else fails:

  • Reach out to your advisor or system admin.
  • Provide job ID, error message, and the job script when asking for help. ```

docs.title: 📘 Glossary
docs.text: This section defines key terms frequently used throughout the REPACSS documentation.

docs.title: A
docs.text:
Account A project allocation or funding unit used to charge for computing time.

Allocation The computing time assigned to a project, typically measured in node-hours.

docs.title: B
docs.text: Batch Job A job submitted to run without user interaction, scheduled by the system.

docs.title: C
docs.text:
Compute Node A server node that runs user jobs and provides computational power.

Container A lightweight, portable software environment used to ensure consistent runtime.

docs.title: G
docs.text: GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) A specialized processor optimized for parallel processing, commonly used in AI and simulations.

docs.title: I
docs.text: Interactive Job A job that allows users to run commands in real time on compute nodes.

docs.title: M
docs.text: Module System Environment management system used to load software dynamically.

docs.title: P
docs.text: Partition A subset of nodes within the cluster grouped together for scheduling (e.g., zen4, h100).

docs.title: S
docs.text:
Slurm The workload manager used by REPACSS to schedule and monitor jobs.

srun A command to launch parallel tasks under Slurm.

salloc Allocates resources interactively under Slurm.

sbatch Submits batch job scripts to the scheduler.

docs.title: 🥮 Basics of Running Jobs
docs.text: REPACSS uses Slurm for cluster/resource management and job scheduling. Slurm is responsible for allocating resources to users, providing a framework for starting, executing and monitoring work on allocated resources, and scheduling work for future execution.

docs.title: 📚 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Slurm Documentation
  • Slurm Tutorials
  • Slurm Manual Pages
  • Slurm FAQ

docs.title: 🧪 Jobs
docs.text:
A job is an allocation of resources such as compute nodes assigned to a user for a specific amount of time. Jobs can be interactive or batch (via script) and may be scheduled for future execution.

Tip

REPACSS provides example job scripts and templates.

When you log in to REPACSS, you land on a login node. Login nodes are meant for preparing jobs (e.g., editing, compiling). Jobs must run on compute nodes, and are submitted using Slurm commands.

The system supports a variety of workflows including interactive tasks, serial runs, GPU applications, and large-scale parallel computations.

docs.title: sbatch
docs.text:
Used to submit a batch job script:

$ sbatch my_job.sh
Submitted batch job 864933

The script should include #SBATCH directives and typically one or more srun commands to launch tasks.

docs.title: salloc
docs.text:
Request resources for an interactive session:

$ salloc --nodes=1 --time=01:00:00 --partition=standard

This opens an interactive shell on a compute node where you can run srun manually.

docs.title: srun
docs.text:
Launches job steps in real time:

$ srun -n 4 ./program

Use inside a job script or interactively to execute code across allocated nodes.

docs.title: ⚙️ Commonly Used Options
docs.text:
Option (long) Short Description sbatch/salloc srun --time -t Max walltime ✅ ❌ --nodes -N Number of compute nodes ✅ ✅ --ntasks -n Total number of MPI tasks ✅ ✅ --cpus-per-task -c Number of CPU cores per task ✅ ✅ --gpus -G Number of GPUs ✅ ✅ --constraint -C Node type or hardware constraint ✅ ❌ --qos -q Quality of service ✅ ❌ --account -A Project account for charging compute time ✅ ❌ --job-name -J Name of the job ✅ ❌

Tip

We recommend using the long-form options (--option=value) in scripts for readability.

docs.title: 📝 Writing a Job Script
docs.text:
Basic format:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=test
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00
#SBATCH --partition=h100
#SBATCH --account=mXXXX

module load gcc

srun -n 4 ./a.out

Scripts should always include required Slurm options. The values can also be passed on the command line:

sbatch -N 2 -p h100 ./job.sh

docs.title: 🤥 Options Inheritance
docs.text:
Slurm options can be declared:

  • in the job script with #SBATCH
  • or on the sbatch command line

If both are present, the command line overrides the script. Many values will automatically propagate to srun via environment variables (e.g., SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES).

However, avoid manual overrides inside scripts unless necessary. Behavior might vary if ssh or manual launch techniques are used.

docs.title: 🐍 Example Script: Python
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=python_job
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00
#SBATCH --partition=zen4

module load gcc
source ~/miniforge3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
conda activate myenv

python script.py

docs.title: ⚠️ Defaults
docs.text:
If not explicitly set, Slurm applies default values:

Option Default nodes 1 time 10 minutes qos debug account default project

Set permanent defaults via environment variables:

export SBATCH_ACCOUNT=m1234
export SALLOC_ACCOUNT=m1234

Warning

Jobs must specify a --constraint (e.g., h100) or they may be rejected.

docs.title: 🎮 Running GPU Jobs
docs.text:
Use --gpus or --gres=gpu:X to request GPU access:

#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1

Failure to do so may cause errors such as:

No CUDA-capable device is detected

Always load the necessary modules:

module load cuda

docs.title: 🔍 Monitoring Jobs
docs.text:
Check status:

squeue -u $USER

Estimate start time:

squeue --start -j <job_id>

Check usage after job completes:

sacct -j <job_id>

docs.title: 🛠️ Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Check quotas: quota -s
  • Verify all required options are included
  • Confirm partition and constraint match available hardware
  • Load appropriate modules before execution
  • Inspect job output files (.out, .err) for errors

docs.title: 🛠️ Best Practices for REPACSS Users
docs.text: This page outlines essential practices for making effective and considerate use of REPACSS resources. Following these practices helps maximize system performance, minimize job delays, and ensure fairness for all users.

docs.title: ✅ General Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use --time Wisely: Always request a realistic --time for your jobs. Overestimating leads to inefficient backfilling; underestimating can cause job termination.

  • Request Only What You Need: Specify appropriate --nodes, --ntasks, and --cpus-per-task. Avoid overprovisioning to reduce idle resource waste.

  • Use Job Arrays: For many similar short jobs, prefer --array to submitting hundreds of independent jobs. This reduces scheduler load.

  • Respect Shared Resources: Avoid launching multiple simultaneous monitoring commands (e.g., watch squeue). Use longer intervals or alternatives like sacct.

  • Automate with Scripts: Use submission and analysis scripts to avoid manual errors and streamline reproducibility.

  • Organize Job Files: Keep your project directory clean by organizing outputs, logs, and scripts into appropriate folders.

docs.title: 🧪 Development vs. Production
docs.text:
• Test First: Use a short wall time and reduced node count to debug your application before scaling up.

  • Scale Gradually: Once the small job completes successfully, increase the node count in stages.

  • Use Interactive Jobs for Debugging: salloc with 1–2 nodes and --time=01:00:00 is ideal for live testing.

  • Track Resource Usage: Use sacct, sstat, and jobstats to analyze completed job efficiency.

docs.title: 🚦 Job Submission Etiquette
docs.text:
• Avoid Resubmitting Frequently: If a job is pending, do not cancel and resubmit repeatedly. This can reduce scheduling efficiency.

  • Use Holds Strategically: Use scontrol hold to pause jobs instead of canceling and resubmitting.

  • Cancel Unused Jobs Promptly: If you no longer need a job or see it stuck in pending unnecessarily, cancel it with scancel.

docs.title: 🔍 Monitoring and Diagnostics
docs.text:
• Log into Compute Nodes: Use scontrol show job <jobid> and SSH into compute nodes for diagnostics while jobs are running.

  • Monitor with Care: Use squeue, sqs, or sacct. If using watch, do so at low frequency: watch -n 60 squeue --me.

  • Enable Email Alerts: Add --mail-type=begin,end,fail and --mail-user=<you@example.com> to scripts for job status notifications.

docs.title: 🧼 Cleanup and Storage
docs.text:
• Purge Temporary Files: Remove scratch files, temporary logs, and unused intermediate outputs after job completion.

  • Archive Results: Regularly move large output files to long-term storage or transfer them to your local system.

  • Limit Disk Footprint: Keep your disk usage under control to prevent quota issues.

docs.title: 🧾 Documentation and Reproducibility
docs.text:
• Document Parameters: Log your job parameters (e.g., SBATCH settings, input files, module loads) to support reproducibility.

  • Version Your Code: Use version control (e.g., Git) and commit job scripts alongside code changes.

  • Include README Files: Explain directory structure, input expectations, and output interpretation in each major folder.

docs.title: 🙌 Collaboration and Support
docs.text:
• Share Responsibly: If collaborating, ensure shared scripts are documented, and avoid hardcoded paths.

  • Use the Ticketing System: If unsure, submit a support ticket with relevant job IDs, error messages, and command history.

  • Stay Updated: Watch for announcements from the system admins about downtime or usage changes.

For more help, visit the Support page.

docs.title: 📄 Example Job Scripts
docs.text:
This page contains structured job script examples tailored for REPACSS. For terminology and scheduler behavior, refer to the Job Basics page. These examples are adapted for REPACSS CPU and GPU partitions like zen4, h100, and standard.

Tip

On REPACSS, interactive jobs in GPU partitions get scheduling priority.

Warning

Each Slurm "cpu" is a hyperthread. Use --cpu-bind=cores to pin OpenMP threads to physical cores.

Note

Use & to run jobs in parallel and wait to synchronize.

docs.title: 💡 Basic MPI Batch Job (CPU)
docs.text:
Use this script to launch a standard MPI-based application across multiple nodes, where each node runs many MPI ranks.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=128
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4

srun ./mpi_app

docs.title: 🔧 Hybrid MPI + OpenMP Job
docs.text:
Hybrid jobs use both MPI for inter-node communication and OpenMP for intra-node threading. Useful for scaling efficiently across sockets.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=2
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00

export OMP_NUM_THREADS=64
srun --cpu-bind=cores ./hybrid_app

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=28
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --time=02:00:00

export OMP_NUM_THREADS=32
srun --cpu-bind=cores ./hybrid_app

docs.title: 🪄 Interactive Jobs
docs.text:
Interactive jobs let you test and debug applications live in a shell on a compute node. Great for prototyping and short runs.

Show Script 
salloc --nodes=1 --ntasks=1 --cpus-per-task=8 --time=01:00:00 --partition=h100

docs.title: 🔀 Sequential Parallel Jobs
docs.text:
This script runs several parallel jobs one after the other. Useful for workflows that require multiple independent steps.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --nodes=4
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4

srun -N 1 ./job1
srun -N 1 ./job2
srun -N 1 ./job3

docs.title: ⬆️ Simultaneous Parallel Jobs
docs.text:
Run several parallel jobs at the same time using background execution. Useful for maximizing resource usage in workflows.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --nodes=8
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4

srun -N 2 -n 176 -c 1 --cpu-bind=cores ./a.out &
srun -N 4 -n 432 -c 1 --cpu-bind=cores ./b.out &
srun -N 2 -n 160 -c 1 --cpu-bind=cores ./c.out &
wait

docs.title: 🚀 GPU Power Capping
docs.text:
Use this script to reduce GPU power usage. Great for energy-aware experiments and optimizing efficiency.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --constraint=h100
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00

nvidia-smi -pl 200
srun ./gpu_app

docs.title: 📆 Job Arrays
docs.text:
Submit many similar jobs at once using a single script. Ideal for parameter sweeps and embarrassingly parallel workloads.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --array=0-2
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --time=00:02:00

echo "Running task ID: $SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID"

docs.title: ⚠️ Job Dependencies
docs.text:
Chain jobs together so one starts after another completes successfully. Useful for pipelining stages.

Show Script 
jobid=$(sbatch --parsable job1.sh)
sbatch --dependency=afterok:$jobid job2.sh

docs.title: 📉 Shared Node Example
docs.text:
Use this when your job doesn’t need an entire node. Helps reduce queue time and resource waste.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --ntasks=2
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=2
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00

srun --cpu-bind=cores ./a.out

docs.title: 💻 Open MPI Example
docs.text:
Use Open MPI via srun after loading the appropriate module. Ensure compatibility with your compiled binaries.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00

module load openmpi
srun ./openmpi_program

docs.title: ⏳ Preemptible Job
docs.text:
This type of job can be interrupted and restarted. Suitable for workflows that can checkpoint or tolerate interruptions.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -C zen4
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --signal=USR1@60 
#SBATCH --requeue

srun ./payload.sh
sleep 120

docs.title: 🔄 MPMD Example
docs.text:
Run multiple executables under the same job using different resource allocations. Used in coupled or multi-app workflows.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --nodes=3
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00

srun -N 1 -n 64 ./a.out : -N 2 -n 32 ./b.out

docs.title: 📅 Job Arrays with Dependencies
docs.text:
Combine job arrays with dependencies for more advanced control over batch workflows.

Show Script 
jobid1=$(sbatch --parsable job1.sh)
jobid2=$(sbatch --parsable --dependency=afterok:$jobid1 job2.sh)
jobid3=$(sbatch --parsable --dependency=afterok:$jobid1 job3.sh)
sbatch --dependency=afterok:$jobid2,afterok:$jobid3 final_job.sh

docs.title: 🤝 Real-Time Job
docs.text:
Submit jobs that need low-latency turnaround, often linked to live experiments or external instruments.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=128
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00

srun ./mycode.exe

docs.title: 📂 Heterogeneous Job
docs.text:
Use this format to request different node types (e.g., CPU + GPU) in a single job.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --time=05:00:00

#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH hetjob
#SBATCH --constraint=h100
#SBATCH --nodes=1

srun --het-group=0 ./cpu_task.sh
srun --het-group=1 ./gpu_task.sh

docs.title: ❌ Overrun Job
docs.text:
Submit a job using the overrun allocation when your project has exceeded its quota. Supports preemption.

Show Script 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --constraint=zen4
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --time-min=01:30:00

srun ./final_job.sh

docs.title: 🎮 Interactive Sessions
docs.text: Interactive sessions allow users to allocate resources in real time to run commands directly on compute nodes. This is useful for debugging, exploratory work, or running graphical applications. REPACSS supports interactive job allocation via the Slurm salloc command.

docs.title: 🚀 Allocating Resources
docs.text:
Use salloc to request an interactive session. This launches a shell on a compute node with the requested resources.

salloc --nodes=1 --ntasks=1 --cpus-per-task=8 --time=01:00:00 --partition=zen4

Or use the shortcut alias available on REPACSS:

interactive -c 8 -p h100

docs.title: 🎛️ Interactive Jobs on GPU Nodes
docs.text:
When working with GPU nodes, make sure to specify GPU resources:

salloc --nodes=1 --gpus=4 --time=01:00:00 --partition=h100

Then, within the session, use srun with GPU flags:

srun --gpus=4 ./my_gpu_program

Warning

If you do not request GPUs with --gpus, CUDA applications may fail with errors like:

no CUDA-capable device is detected

docs.title: 🖥️ Interactive Jobs on CPU Nodes
docs.text:
To run on a CPU-only partition:

salloc --nodes=1 --ntasks=1 --cpus-per-task=8 --time=01:00:00 --partition=zen4

Use the shell to run commands interactively or launch parallel tasks with srun:

srun ./my_cpu_program

docs.title: ⏱️ Wait Limits and Timeout
docs.text:
Interactive jobs will be canceled if resources are not allocated within 6 minutes. To change this behavior, use the --immediate flag:

# Wait up to 10 minutes (600 seconds) for resources
salloc --nodes=1 --time=01:00:00 --partition=zen4 --immediate=600

docs.title: ⚠️ Common Errors
docs.text:
• Missing GPUs in srun: Always specify GPU count in both salloc and srun.
  • Account Errors: Ensure you have a valid default account or specify one with --account=<project>.
  • No Resources Available: Try a different partition or reduce requested time/nodes.

docs.title: 📚 Related Pages
docs.text:
• Running Jobs
  • Queues
  • Example Job Scripts

docs.title: Monitoring Jobs
docs.text:
Note

Avoid running multiple instances of watch squeue or watch sqs. This can overload the scheduler, which is a shared system resource. If you must use watch, do watch -n 60 and stop the process when you're done.

docs.title: 🔍 Using squeue
docs.text:
squeue shows job queue information directly from Slurm. It's useful for checking job status like PENDING, RUNNING, or COMPLETED.

squeue --me          # Shows your jobs
squeue -u $USER      # Equivalent to --me
squeue --me -t R     # Only running jobs
squeue --me -t PD    # Only pending jobs
squeue -j 1234,1235  # Filter by job IDs

To show jobs by account:

squeue -A your_project_name

To view job steps:

squeue --steps 1001.0

docs.title: 📋 Using sacct
docs.text:
sacct displays accounting information for active and completed jobs.

Basic usage:

sacct

Format output by fields:

sacct --format=JobID,JobName,State,Start,Elapsed

Filter by date range:

sacct -S 2024-06-01 -E 2024-06-13

Show failed jobs:

sacct -X --format=User,JobName,State -s F --start=2024-06-01 --end=now

Filter by job ID:

sacct -j 123456,123457

docs.title: 📈 Using sstat
docs.text:
sstat reports resource usage for running jobs:

sstat -j 123456 -o JobID,MaxRSS

docs.title: 📊 Using jobstats
docs.text:
jobstats (Python-based tool) gives a summary of job activity from sacct, squeue, and sreport.

module load python
jobstats

Example:

jobstats --user elvis --start 2024-06-01 --end 2024-06-13

To view options:

jobstats --help

docs.title: 📮 Email Notifications
docs.text:
Add these lines to your job script to get notified:

#SBATCH --mail-type=begin,end,fail
#SBATCH --mail-user=your@email.com

docs.title: 🧠 Logging into Compute Nodes
docs.text:
To SSH into a node while your job is running:

  1. Find your job's nodes:
scontrol show job <jobid> | grep -oP 'NodeList=nid(\[.+\]|.+)'
  1. SSH into any of the listed nodes:
ssh nid000123

To get the head node:

scontrol show job <jobid> | grep -oP 'BatchHost=\K\w+'

Note

SSH access only works while the job is running.

docs.title: 🧹 Updating or Canceling Jobs
docs.text:
Cancel job:

scancel 123456

Cancel multiple jobs:

scancel 123456 123457

Cancel all your jobs:

scancel -u $USER

Update job time:

scontrol update jobid=123456 timelimit=02:00:00

docs.title: ⛔ Holding or Releasing Jobs
docs.text:
Hold a job (pause scheduling):

scontrol hold 123456

Release a held job:

scontrol release 123456

Requeue (rerun) a job:

scontrol requeue 123456

docs.title: 🧾 Job Queues on REPACSS
docs.text: This page explains how job queues work on REPACSS and how users can effectively navigate the system to manage their job submissions.

docs.title: 🧵 What Is a Queue?
docs.text:
When you submit a job on REPACSS, it is placed in a queue managed by the Slurm workload manager. This queue operates on a priority-based system, meaning jobs are sorted and run based on several factors such as available resources, job size, and scheduling policies.

Think of the queue like a line for a theme park ride—everyone waits their turn, but some might have express passes or meet special conditions that allow them to go sooner. All jobs, regardless of their type, eventually run on the same compute nodes, but some may have shorter wait times based on priority.

docs.title: 🕒 Queue Wait Times
docs.text:
Jobs may wait in the queue for longer than their actual runtime. This is a normal part of using a shared HPC system. The wait time depends on:

  • System load
  • Resource availability (number of nodes, CPUs, GPUs, etc.)
  • Job size and walltime requested
  • Scheduling priority

If your job is small or flexible (for example, it can run on shared nodes or has a short walltime), it may start sooner.

docs.title: 📋 Viewing the Queue
docs.text:
To check your job’s position and queue status, use the squeue command:

squeue -u $USER

This will show all your jobs and their current status (e.g., pending, running). You can also estimate start times with:

squeue --start -j <job_id>

If your job is pending, Slurm will show the reason (e.g., Resources, Priority, Dependency) in the NODELIST(REASON) column.

docs.title: ✅ Job Scheduling Tips
docs.text:
• Submit jobs with accurate resource and time estimates
  • Avoid over-requesting nodes or time
  • Use shared or interactive modes for smaller or test jobs
  • Consider breaking up large jobs into smaller chunks
  • Use job arrays when submitting many similar jobs

docs.title: 🧠 Understanding Job States
docs.text:
Here are the most common job states you’ll encounter:

State Description PENDING (PD) Job is waiting for resources to become available RUNNING (R) Job is currently executing on allocated nodes COMPLETED (CD) Job finished successfully FAILED (F) Job terminated with an error CANCELLED (CA) Job was cancelled by the user or admin TIMEOUT (TO) Job ran out of the requested walltime

You can check completed jobs using:

sacct -u $USER --starttime today

docs.title: 🔍 Need Help?
docs.text:
If you’re unsure why your job is not starting, or want help with optimizing your job submission, contact the system administrators or open a support ticket.

Use the --help flag with any Slurm command to learn more:

squeue --help
sacct --help

For further help, refer to:

  • Job Basics
  • Example Scripts
  • Monitoring Tools

docs.title: 🧾 Job Queues on REPACSS
docs.text: This page explains how job queues work on REPACSS and how users can effectively navigate the system to manage their job submissions.

docs.title: 🧵 What Is a Queue?
docs.text:
When you submit a job on REPACSS, it is placed in a queue managed by the Slurm workload manager. This queue operates on a priority-based system, meaning jobs are sorted and run based on several factors such as available resources, job size, and scheduling policies.

Think of the queue like a line for a theme park ride—everyone waits their turn, but some might have express passes or meet special conditions that allow them to go sooner. All jobs, regardless of their type, eventually run on the same compute nodes, but some may have shorter wait times based on priority.

docs.title: 🕒 Queue Wait Times
docs.text:
Jobs may wait in the queue for longer than their actual runtime. This is a normal part of using a shared HPC system. The wait time depends on:

  • System load
  • Resource availability (number of nodes, CPUs, GPUs, etc.)
  • Job size and walltime requested
  • Scheduling priority

If your job is small or flexible (for example, it can run on shared nodes or has a short walltime), it may start sooner.

docs.title: 📋 Viewing the Queue
docs.text:
To check your job’s position and queue status, use the squeue command:

squeue -u $USER

This will show all your jobs and their current status (e.g., pending, running). You can also estimate start times with:

squeue --start -j <job_id>

If your job is pending, Slurm will show the reason (e.g., Resources, Priority, Dependency) in the NODELIST(REASON) column.

docs.title: ⏲️ Job Scheduling
docs.text: REPACSS uses the Slurm workload manager to determine when jobs are run. The scheduler’s goal is to keep the system as busy as possible while maximizing job throughput. Scheduling decisions are based on factors like job size, requested time, and priority.

docs.title: 🔼 Priority and Aging
docs.text:
Each job in the queue is assigned a priority value. Over time, this value increases in a process known as aging. REPACSS ages jobs at a fixed rate (e.g., 1 point per minute), and only a limited number of jobs per user are allowed to age at any given time.

This prioritization ensures that older jobs don’t get stuck indefinitely. However, only the top few jobs per user actively age to maintain fairness across the system.

docs.title: ⚙️ Scheduling Algorithms
docs.text:
Slurm uses two types of scheduling:

  • Immediate Scheduler: Quickly builds a tentative schedule from the highest-priority jobs.
  • Backfill Scheduler: Tries to fill in the gaps with smaller jobs that can run without delaying higher-priority ones.

This combination allows REPACSS to maintain high utilization while ensuring that shorter and flexible jobs aren’t delayed unnecessarily. The schedule is rebuilt frequently, which is why start times shown in squeue --start can change often.

docs.title: ✅ Job Scheduling Tips
docs.text:
• Submit jobs with accurate resource and time estimates
  • Avoid over-requesting nodes or time
  • Use shared or interactive modes for smaller or test jobs
  • Consider breaking up large jobs into smaller chunks
  • Use job arrays when submitting many similar jobs

docs.title: 🧠 Understanding Job States
docs.text:
Here are the most common job states you’ll encounter:

State Description PENDING (PD) Job is waiting for resources to become available RUNNING (R) Job is currently executing on allocated nodes COMPLETED (CD) Job finished successfully FAILED (F) Job terminated with an error CANCELLED (CA) Job was cancelled by the user or admin TIMEOUT (TO) Job ran out of the requested walltime

You can check completed jobs using:

sacct -u $USER --starttime today

docs.text:
If you’re unsure why your job is not starting, or want help with optimizing your job submission, contact the system administrators or open a support ticket.

Use the --help flag with any Slurm command to learn more:

squeue --help
sacct --help

For further help, refer to:

  • Job Basics

docs.title: Available Software on REPACSS
docs.text: This document provides a formal summary of software environments currently available on the REPACSS high-performance computing system. Software is managed and accessed using the environment module framework, which allows users to dynamically configure their shell environments to access compilers, libraries, and applications relevant to their research or instructional activities.

docs.title: Software Module Locations
docs.text: Software modules are organized within predefined repository paths. The following modules are currently available for loading and execution:

docs.title: /opt/apps/nfs/modules/zen4/rocky9.4/openmpi/4.1.6-vhun5qt/Core
docs.text:
• fftw/3.3.10 Fast Fourier Transform library used in signal and image processing applications. sss
  • hdf5/1.10.11 Hierarchical Data Format library for managing large and complex data collections.

docs.title: /opt/apps/nfs/modules/zen4/rocky9.4/Core
docs.text:
• gcc/14.2.0 GNU Compiler Collection supporting C, C++, and Fortran languages.

  • openmpi/4.1.6 High-performance implementation of the Message Passing Interface (MPI) standard.

Note

The (L) designation indicates that the module is currently loaded in the user’s active environment.

docs.title: Guidance for Module Usage
docs.text: Users are advised to utilize the following commands for listing and managing software modules:

docs.title: Display All Available Modules
docs.text: module avail

docs.title: Display Only Default Modules
docs.text:
In the case of an extensive list, users may restrict output to default modules:

module --default avail
# or
ml -d av

docs.title: View Module Categories and Quantities
docs.text:
To obtain a summary overview of available module categories and counts:

module overview
# or
ml ov

docs.title: Discover All Installed Modules and Extensions
docs.text:
To enumerate all possible software packages and extensions, regardless of default visibility:

module spider

docs.title: Loading Software Modules
docs.text:
To access a software environment, load the corresponding module. For example:

module load gcc/14.2.0

To verify currently loaded modules:

module list

To unload a module:

module unload <module_name>

docs.title: Additional Support and Documentation
docs.text:
Users seeking more detailed instructions for using the module system are encouraged to consult the Module System User Guide.

If software needed for your research or coursework is not listed among available modules, you may submit a request to the REPACSS support team for consideration.

For further assistance, please contact:

REPACSS Support
repacss.support@ttu.edu

docs.title: 📦 Installing Packages
docs.text: This page provides guidance for installing additional software or Python packages in the REPACSS environment. Users can install packages in their own workspace without administrative privileges.

docs.title: 🐍 Python Packages
docs.text: Python 3.11 is available via Miniforge and supports both conda and pip for managing packages.

docs.title: ✅ Activate Miniforge
docs.text:
First, activate your base Python environment:

source ~/miniforge3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
conda activate base

If conda is not found, ensure Miniforge is installed in your home directory. Contact support if not available.

docs.title: 📥 Installing with Conda
docs.text:
Install packages into a named environment:

conda create -n myenv numpy scipy matplotlib
conda activate myenv

To add more packages later:

conda install pandas scikit-learn

docs.title: 📥 Installing with Pip
docs.text:
If your package is not available via conda:

pip install somepackage

You can install from a requirements.txt file:

pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 💻 C/C++ or Fortran Libraries
docs.text:
If you need to install custom libraries (e.g., building from source):

  1. Load the appropriate modules:
module load gcc/12.2.0 openmpi/4.1.5
  1. Build and install:
./configure --prefix=$HOME/mylibs
make -j
make install

Add your installation path to the environment:

export PATH="$HOME/mylibs/bin:$PATH"
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH="$HOME/mylibs/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH"

docs.title: ⚠️ Tips
docs.text:
• Avoid installing packages system-wide. Use local directories (e.g., $HOME, $WORK) for all custom installs.
  • Use conda environments to keep package dependencies isolated and manageable.
  • Consider using virtual environments or containers (e.g., conda, venv, or Apptainer) for reproducibility.

docs.title: 📚 Related Pages
docs.text:
• Module System
  • Available Software
  • Python Environment Setup

docs.title: 📦 Module System
docs.text: REPACSS uses the Lmod module system to manage software environments. Modules allow users to dynamically modify their shell environment by loading and unloading installed software packages.

docs.title: 🔍 Checking Available Modules
docs.text:
To see all available software modules:

module avail

You can search for a specific module by name:

module avail python

docs.title: 🕷️ Using module spider
docs.text:
module spider offers advanced search capabilities and provides information about module versions and dependencies.

Search for all matching modules:

module spider python

To get detailed info about a specific version:

module spider python/3.11.0

docs.title: 📥 Loading a Module
docs.text:
To use a software package, load its module:

module load gcc/12.2.0
module load openmpi/4.1.5

You can load multiple modules at once:

module load gcc/12.2.0 cuda/12.0

docs.title: 📤 Unloading a Module
docs.text:
To unload a specific module:

module unload gcc/12.2.0

Unload all loaded modules:

module purge

docs.title: 📄 Viewing Module Details
docs.text:
To inspect the environment changes a module will make:

module show hdf5

docs.title: 🧾 Listing Loaded Modules
docs.text:
To see which modules you have currently loaded:

module list

docs.title: 💡 Tips
docs.text:
• Load required modules in your job scripts before launching your application.
  • Use module purge at the top of scripts to ensure a clean environment.
  • If you encounter conflicts, try purging and reloading modules.

docs.title: 📚 Learn More
docs.text:
Visit the Available Software page for a list of software provided on REPACSS.

For Python-related setups, see Installing Packages.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
REPACSS offers Python via the module system, but for better control and reproducibility, users are encouraged to set up their own conda environments using Miniforge.

Miniforge is a minimal installer for Conda that defaults to the conda-forge channel. It is recommended because:

  • ✅ Community-driven with up-to-date packages
  • 🚫 No reliance on the defaults channel (avoids conflicts)
  • 🪶 Lightweight footprint
  • 🔄 Better compatibility with HPC and scientific libraries

docs.title: 🔧 Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Access to REPACSS login nodes
  • Familiarity with the Linux shell
  • Basic understanding of Python packaging

docs.title: 🧹 Removing Old Conda Installations
docs.text:
Before installing Miniforge, remove any previous Conda installations to avoid conflicts.

conda --version     # If not found, you're good to go

ls -al | grep conda # Check for conda files
find . -maxdepth 1 -name "*conda*" -exec rm -ir {} +

docs.title: 📥 Installing Miniforge
docs.text:
1. Download the installer: 

    wget https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge/releases/latest/download/Miniforge3-Linux-x86_64.sh

  2. Make it executable and run: 

    chmod +x Miniforge3-Linux-x86_64.sh
./Miniforge3-Linux-x86_64.sh

  3. Choose /home/$USER/miniforge3 as the install path.

  4. Source your shell config: 

    source ~/.bashrc

docs.title: 🔧 Managing Environments
docs.text:
conda create -n myenv python=3.11     # Create
conda activate myenv                  # Activate
conda install numpy pandas scipy      # Install packages
conda deactivate                      # Deactivate

Use pip for additional packages as needed.

docs.title: 🧪 Environment Management
docs.text:
# Export current environment
conda env export > environment.yml

# Re-create from file
conda env create -f environment.yml
  • Keep environments separate per project
  • Avoid modifying the base environment
  • Update Conda regularly: 
    conda update conda

docs.title: 💾 Storage Tips
docs.text:
• Install environments in $HOME
  • Use $SCRATCH for datasets or large files
  • Clean unused envs: 
    conda env remove -n old_env

docs.title: 🧑‍💻 Interactive Mode
docs.text:
interactive -c 8 -p h100
conda activate myenv
python script.py

docs.title: 🗂 Batch Script
docs.text:
Create run_python.sh:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=python_job
#SBATCH --output=python_job.out
#SBATCH --error=python_job.err
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00
#SBATCH --partition=h100
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G

module load gcc

source ~/miniforge3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
conda activate myenv

python script.py

Submit it: 

sbatch run_python.sh

docs.title: ❌ Environment Doesn’t Activate
docs.text:
Tip

Miniforge might not be sourced. Run: 

source ~/miniforge3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh

docs.title: 📦 Package Won’t Install
docs.text:
Tip

Use the conda-forge channel: 

conda install -c conda-forge package_name

docs.title: 🧠 Memory Errors
docs.text:
Note

  • Adjust memory in your #SBATCH --mem line
  • Monitor usage with htop or sacct

docs.title: REPACSS Architecture
docs.text: Welcome to the architectural overview of REPACSS — the Research and Educational Platform for Advanced Computing Support Services at Texas Tech University. This document will guide you through the compute, storage, and networking resources available on the REPACSS cluster.

docs.title: 🧠 Compute Resources
docs.text: REPACSS is a heterogeneous high-performance computing system optimized for both traditional CPU workloads and modern GPU-accelerated applications.

docs.title: CPU Compute Nodes
docs.text:
The majority of REPACSS's compute capacity comes from high-core-count AMD EPYC CPUs, suitable for large parallel jobs and memory-intensive simulations.

  • 110 CPU nodes
  • Dual AMD EPYC 9754 processors
  • 256 total cores per node
  • 1.5 TB DDR5 memory
  • 1.92 TB local NVMe SSD

docs.title: GPU Compute Nodes
docs.text:
GPU resources are designed for machine learning, data analytics, and other massively parallel applications.

  • 8 GPU nodes
  • Dual Intel Xeon Gold 6448Y CPUs (64 cores)
  • 512 GB RAM per node
  • 4 × NVIDIA H100 NVL GPUs (94 GB HBM each)
  • 1.92 TB local SSD

docs.title: Login Nodes
docs.text:
Users interact with the system through login nodes, where editing, compiling, and job submissions take place.

  • 3 login nodes
  • Dual AMD EPYC 9254 CPUs (48 cores)
  • 256 GB RAM
  • 1.92 TB NVMe SSD

docs.title: 💾 Storage Resources
docs.text: REPACSS has nearly 3 PB of storage capacity distributed across multiple tiers optimized for different use cases.

docs.title: Tiered Storage Overview
docs.text:
• Home: Persistent personal storage for source code and config files.
  • Scratch: High-performance temporary storage for active simulations. Subject to purge policy.
  • Archive: Long-term storage for research results and finalized datasets.

docs.title: 🌐 Network Architecture
docs.text:
The REPACSS network architecture is designed to deliver high bandwidth and low latency for compute and storage operations.

  • Core interconnect: Dell PowerSwitch S5248-ON and S5232-ON
  • Storage network: Dedicated high-throughput fabric
  • High-speed NICs support multi-node parallelism and MPI workloads

The network topology ensures: - Efficient data movement between compute and storage - Minimized I/O bottlenecks during job execution - Reliable system access for remote users

docs.title: 🗃 Storage Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use $HOME for configs, scripts, and small files
  • Use $SCRATCH for intermediate output and I/O-heavy workloads
  • Archive finalized results in the long-term storage pool

Need more performance?

Running I/O intensive jobs? Always use $SCRATCH to avoid hitting I/O bottlenecks.

Don't forget to archive!

Scratch space is periodically purged. Move important data to long-term storage to avoid data loss.
